title: Fougasse
subtitle: Provençal leaf-shaped flatbread with olive oil
excerpt: Fougasse is the Provençal flatbread shaped and cut to resemble a leaf or sheaf of wheat, then baked until the edges are thin and crisp and the interior stays open and soft. Olive oil goes into the dough and over the top before it goes in the oven. The dramatic shape requires only a bench scraper and a pair of scissors.
sourceNotes: Fougasse descends from the Roman panis focacius, a flatbread cooked on the hearth. In Provence the distinctive leaf shape is formed by cutting slashes through the dough before baking, which allows the bread to expand dramatically and produces thin, crackling edges around an open crumb. Regional variations include fougasse aux olives, aux lardons, and aux herbes de Provence. This recipe follows the plain olive oil version typical of the Languedoc coast.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be mixed and left to bulk ferment in the fridge overnight. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before shaping.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; bake on a preheated tray for maximum oven-spring; 15 to 20 minutes until the thin edges are deep gold and crisp

content.text: Fougasse is shaped by cutting, not moulding. Once the dough has had its bulk ferment, you flatten it onto a tray, then use a bench scraper or scissors to cut a central spine and several angled branches through the dough. Pull the cuts open slightly; they will expand further in the oven. The result is a loaf that bakes unevenly by design: thin, crackling edges and a soft, open interior.

Combine 350 g strong white flour, 2 g fast-action yeast, and 7 g salt in a large bowl. Add 250 ml lukewarm water and 30 ml olive oil. Mix with your hand until no dry flour remains, then work the dough for 3 to 4 minutes until smooth and slightly tacky. Cover and leave to bulk ferment at room temperature for 60 to 90 minutes until visibly puffed.

Heat the oven to 230 degrees C fan with a baking tray inside. Divide the dough in two. Working on a lightly oiled surface, stretch each piece gently into an oval roughly 30 cm long and 20 cm wide. Transfer each piece to a sheet of baking paper. Using a bench scraper or scissors, cut one central slash down the length of the oval, then three or four angled cuts on each side like the branches of a leaf. Pull the cuts open with your fingers so they are at least 2 cm wide. Rest for 20 to 25 minutes.

Brush the tops generously with olive oil and scatter over flaked salt and rosemary leaves if using. Slide onto the hot baking tray using the baking paper. Bake for 15 to 20 minutes until the thin edges are deep gold and crisp and the thicker areas are cooked through. They will continue to crisp as they cool. Serve warm, torn rather than sliced.

items.problem: The cuts closed up during baking
items.solution: The cuts were not opened wide enough, or the dough was too tight. Pull the slashes open to at least 2 to 3 cm before baking; they close slightly in the oven, so start wider than seems necessary.

items.problem: The fougasse is doughy in the thick areas
items.solution: The oval was too thick. Stretch the dough to no more than 1.5 cm at its thickest point. If baking both sheets at once, rotate them halfway through so both get direct heat from the hot tray.
